name: Pusher Channels / Pusher Beams
what_it_does: Pusher Channels provides hosted WebSocket infrastructure for real-time pub/sub messaging between servers and browser/mobile clients. Pusher Beams provides push notification delivery to iOS, Android, and web browsers. Together they cover real-time in-app messaging and background push notifications.
use_cases: Pushing live agent status updates and streaming output to a web dashboard without polling
Broadcasting events from a server-side agent to all connected browser clients on a shared channel
Implementing presence channels to track which users or agent workers are currently active
Triggering client-side UI updates from an agent pipeline when a background job completes
Sending push notifications to mobile devices or browsers when agents complete async tasks
not_for: Durable message queuing or guaranteed delivery to offline consumers (use SQS, RabbitMQ, or a queue service)
High-frequency machine-to-machine messaging between backend services at scale (use Ably or Kafka)
Video or audio streaming and WebRTC (use LiveKit, Daily, or Agora)
best_when: You need a simple, well-documented WebSocket layer to push server-side events to browser or mobile clients without managing WebSocket infrastructure yourself.
avoid_when: You need message persistence, guaranteed delivery, offline replay, or complex routing logic beyond simple channel subscriptions.

auth.notes: App key and secret used for server-side publishing via REST API. Private and presence channels require a server-side auth endpoint that signs channel authorization requests using HMAC. Public channels require no auth. Beams uses a separate instance ID and secret key. No OAuth or fine-grained permission scopes.

pricing.free_tier_limits: Sandbox: 200 max concurrent connections, 200,000 messages/day, unlimited channels
pricing.paid_tiers: Starter $49/mo; Growth $99/mo; Scale $299/mo — scales on connections and message volume
pricing.notes: Daily message limit on free tier can be restrictive for high-frequency agents. Connection limit is per app, not per channel. Beams (push notifications) has a separate free tier of up to 1,000 monthly active devices.

performance.rate_limits: 10 messages/sec per channel on free tier; REST publish API: 100 events per batch call, 10 channels per event trigger

agent_readiness.error_message_notes: HTTP API returns standard status codes with JSON error bodies. SDK errors are generally descriptive. Channel auth failures return 403 with a message but limited diagnostic detail on what caused the auth endpoint to fail.
agent_readiness.idempotency_notes: No idempotency keys supported. Pusher does not deduplicate published messages. Duplicate event detection and handling is entirely the agent's responsibility.
known_agent_gotchas: Private and presence channels require a live auth endpoint on the agent's server — if that endpoint is unavailable, all private channel subscriptions fail
The 100-event batch publish limit applies per API call — agents publishing high-frequency events must implement batching logic
Messages are not persisted; clients that disconnect and reconnect will miss events published during the gap
Channel names are case-sensitive and have a 200-character limit — agents generating channel names dynamically must enforce this constraint
Free tier hard daily message cap resets at midnight UTC — agents must implement fallback behavior or graceful degradation when the cap is hit
Pusher Beams (push notifications) and Pusher Channels are separate products with separate credentials and SDKs

security_score_components.security_notes: Channel authorization tokens are HMAC-signed server-side — app secret never reaches clients. App secret must be protected server-side. No fine-grained scope system beyond public/private/presence channel types. SOC2 Type II certified.

score_rationale: Pusher Channels is well-documented and straightforward to integrate for server-to-client real-time event delivery. Agent readiness is good for the outbound publish use case (trigger event → clients receive it). The main limitations are no message persistence, no idempotency, the channel auth endpoint dependency for private channels, and the daily message cap on free tier. Best choice when agents need to stream output to browser dashboards or notify clients of async task completion.
